visual: Picture Graphs (side-by-side comparison). Brief callback — same data as 1:2 and 1:5 picture graphs (both vertical). Key highlighted on 1:5 graph: "Each ⭐ = 5." Data Table not visible.
guide: "In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale-of-5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE."
guide_2: "Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?"
prompt: "What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 2, 5, 10, 1]
on_correct: "Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale."
remediation_light: "Check the key. It says 'Each symbol = 5.' That's the scale: 5."

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). "Favorite Playground Activities" — Swings: 20, Slides: 15, Monkey Bars: 25, Sandbox: 10. Vertical.
visual_2: Picture Graphs (Grouping Animation). Items appear for Swings (20 items). Key visible: "Each ⭐ = 5." Animation: 20 items → 4 groups of 5 → 4 symbols stacking on graph. Guide narrates skip-counting.
guide: "Let's see how 20 items become symbols when the scale is 5. Watch—we'll group by 5s."
animation_sequence:
auto-play, ~45 seconds:
  - 20 items appear
  - Items group: 5, 5, 5, 5 (four groups form)
  - Guide counts aloud: "5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 groups of 5."
  - Groups transform into 4 symbols stacking upward on picture graph
  - "20 items = 4 symbols. Each symbol shows 5."
on_complete: Picture graph shows Swings column with 4 symbols. Other columns empty.

visual: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Swings column: 4 symbols (from animation). Other columns empty. Key visible: "Each ⭐ = 5."
visual_2: Data Tables (Numeric Display). Same data visible alongside
guide: "It is easier to read this picture graph if you can count by 5s. That's exactly what we do with scale of 5."
guide_2: "Let's check: does the picture graph match our data table? 4 symbols, scale of 5. Count by 5s with me… 4 symbols, counting by 5s. What's the total?"
prompt: "What is the total represented by the picture graph?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 4, 9, 15, 20]
answer_rationale:
20 = Correct (5, 10, 15, 20)
  - 4 = Error: Counted symbols only (Misconception #1)
  - 9 = Error: Added 5+4 (Misconception #2)
  - 15 = Counting error (stopped at 3 groups)
on_correct: "20 items. 5, 10, 15, 20. Skip-counting tells you the total in the picture graph and that matches the value in the data table."

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). Full data visible. Vertical.
visual_2: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Swings = 4 symbols (pre-filled from animation). Slides, Monkey Bars, Sandbox empty. Key visible.
guide: "Slides has 15. How many symbols is that?"
guide_2: "Think: count by 5s until you reach 15. How many 5s? Click to set the height of the Slides."
prompt: "Set the number of symbols for Slides."
student_action: Hover over Slides column to see preview fill, click to set at 3 symbols
on_correct: "3 symbols. 5, 10, 15. You used skip-counting to figure it out."

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). Same data. Vertical.
visual_2: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Swings = 4, Slides = 3 (student-created). Monkey Bars, Sandbox empty. Key visible.
guide: "Monkey Bars has 25. That's a bigger number. Click to set the height of the Monkey Bars."
prompt: "Set the height for Monkey Bars."
student_action: Hover to preview, click to set at 5 symbols
on_correct: "5 symbols for 25. You counted: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25."

visual: Same vertical picture graph. Swings=4 symbols (from animation), Slides=3 symbols, Monkey Bars=5 symbols (just placed). Sandbox column empty. Data table remains visible.
guide: "Let's complete the picture graph. Sandbox has 10. That's the smallest number. Click to set the height for Sandbox."
prompt: "Set the height for Sandbox."
student_action: Hover to preview, click to set at 2 symbols
on_correct: "2 symbols for 10. Count by 5s: 5, 10."

visual: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Completed graph: Swings = 4, Slides = 3, Monkey Bars = 5, Sandbox = 2. Key visible: "Each ⭐ = 5." Data Table NOT visible.
guide: You made a scaled picture graph. Let's read it. Which activity is the most popular?
prompt: Click on the activity that is most popular.
on_correct: Monkey Bars has 5 symbols, that's 25. The tallest column has the most votes.
prompt_2: Click on the category with the fewest.
on_correct_2: Sandbox has only 2 symbols, that's 10 votes.

visual: Picture graph from Section 1 remains visible on left side of screen.
guide: "You made a picture graph with scale of 5. Now let's see ANOTHER way to show the exact same data."

visual: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Completed "Favorite Playground Activities" graph from Section 1 (vertical). Swings=4 symbols, Slides=3, Monkey Bars=5, Sandbox=2. Key visible: "Each ⭐ = 5."
visual_2: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). SAME data shown as bar graph (vertical). Categories: Swings=20, Slides=15, Monkey Bars=25, Sandbox=10. Axis labeled 0–30 in 5s. All 4 bars pre-filled. Side-by-side with picture graph.
visual_3: Data Tables (Numeric Display). Same "Playground Activities" data visible alongside both graphs.
guide: "You've read bar graphs before. Here's the same Playground data as a bar graph."
guide_2: "In a picture graph, you count by 5s using SYMBOLS: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25."
guide_3: "In a bar graph, you count by 5s using the numbers written along the side of the graph on the AXIS. Look—5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting, just written on the side. Both graphs count by 5s. Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?"
prompt: "Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: "In the symbols" / "On the axis" / "In the title"]
on_correct: "The axis shows the skip-counting: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's your scale of 5—built right into the side of the graph."

visual: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Same side-by-side display from 2.1 (picture graph + bar graph). Slides category highlighted on both graphs. Bar height = 15, picture graph = 3 symbols. Data Table NOT visible.
guide: "In a bar graph, you read the HEIGHT, where the bar ENDS on the axis. Look at Slides. What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar? Where does it end?"
prompt: "What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 3, 10, 15, 20]
answer_rationale:
15 = Correct (bar height ends at 15 mark)
3 = Error: Counted symbols from picture graph (Misconception #1)
10 = Misread axis
20 = Misread axis
on_correct: "15. The bar's HEIGHT is 15. It ends at 15 on the axis. Same as 3 symbols on the picture graph. Both show 15."
remediation_light: "Find the HEIGHT: where the top of the bar lines up with the numbers on the side, the axis."

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). "Favorite Lunch Foods" — Pizza=20, Tacos=30, Salad=15, Pasta=25, Sandwiches=15. 5 categories. Vertical.
visual_2: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Axis labeled 0–35 in 5s. Tacos bar pre-filled at 30, Sandwiches bar pre-filled at 15. Pizza, Salad, Pasta bars empty. Axis numbers highlighted (for axis-reading question). Click-to-height.
guide: "Before you create the bars, look at the numbers on the axis."
guide_2: "These numbers count by 5s—just like you did with symbols. The axis does the skip-counting for you. What comes after 20 on this axis?"
prompt: "What comes after 20 on this axis?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 21, 25, 30, 22]
on_correct: "25. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30... Same skip-counting you used for symbols, just written on the side."

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). Same "Favorite Lunch Foods" data visible.
visual_2: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Tacos=30 (pre-filled), Sandwiches=15 (pre-filled). Pizza, Salad, Pasta bars empty. Click-to-height.
guide: "Pizza has 20 votes. You found 20 on the axis. Set the height for Pizza to show 20."
prompt: "Set the height for Pizza to show 20."
student_action: Hover over Pizza bar to see preview, click to set height at 20
on_correct: "The bar ends at 20—right on the axis. The skip-counting is already there; you just match the height."
remediation_light: "Find 20 on the axis. Click when the bar reaches that mark."

visual: Same vertical bar graph. Pizza now at 20. Tacos pre-filled at 30. Sandwiches pre-filled at 15. Salad and Pasta bars empty.
guide: "Finish the graph. Salad has 15, Pasta has 25. Set the heights for Salad and Pasta."
prompt: "Set the heights for Salad and Pasta."
student_action: Hover and click to set Salad=15, Pasta=25
on_correct: "You built the bar graph. Each bar shows exactly how many votes that food got."
remediation_light: "Check the table for each food's number. Find that number on the axis on the side and click at that height."

visual: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Completed "Favorite Lunch Foods" bar graph (Pizza=20, Tacos=30, Salad=15, Pasta=25, Sandwiches=15). Vertical picture graph version appears alongside (same data, scale of 5). Key visible: "Each ⭐ = 5." Data Table visible.
guide: "Here's the same data as a picture graph. Let's check: do they match? Look at Pasta. The bar shows 25. How many symbols should the picture graph have?"
prompt: "How many symbols should the picture graph have?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 4, 5, 20, 25]
answer_rationale:
5 = Correct (count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 — that's 5 fives)
  - 25 = Error: Confused value with symbol count
  - 4 = Calculation error
  - 20 = Misread
on_correct: "5 symbols. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. There are 5 fives. Bar graph and picture graph: same information, different format."
remediation_light: "Each symbol shows 5. Count by 5s until you reach 25. How many times?"

visual: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Vertical. Completed "Favorite Lunch Foods" bar graph. Tacos (30) and Salad (15) bars highlighted. Data Table and picture graph NOT visible.
guide: "You already know how to compare using graphs. Same skill works here. How many MORE students chose Tacos than Salad?"
prompt: "How many MORE students chose Tacos than Salad?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 10, 15, 30, 45]
answer_rationale:
15 = Correct (30 - 15 = 15)
  - 10 = Calculation error
  - 30 = Used Tacos value only
  - 45 = Added instead of subtracted (Misconception #6)
on_correct: "15 more. Tacos has 30, Salad has 15. The difference is 15."
remediation_light: "How many MORE means find the difference. 30 minus 15 is..."
divider:
→ SECTION 2 COMPLETE. PROCEED TO SECTION 3.

---

### **1.7.3 LESSON SECTION 3: Full Independence — Both Formats**

**Purpose:** Remove scaffolding. Students create full graphs from numeric Data Tables without animation support. Larger values (25-40 range). Students demonstrate they can work with both formats independently. Horizontal orientation proves flexibility.

**Orientation:** All horizontal (Guide acknowledges switch)

---

unresolved: Data Table and picture graph NOT

visual: Previous graphs clear. Data Table appears (numeric only).
guide: "So far, some parts of the graphs were already filled in for you. Now let's see if you can build a whole graph on your own."

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). "Books Read This Month" — Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40. 4 categories. Vertical Data Table layout.
visual_2: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Horizontal. 0 categories pre-filled. Key visible: "Each ⭐ = 5 books."
guide: "This graph goes sideways—horizontal. Same idea, you just click for the length for the number of symbols instead of height."
guide_2: "Make a picture graph. Start with Liam. He read 35 books. Set the number of symbols for Liam."
prompt: "Set the number of symbols for Liam."
student_action: Hover to preview symbols extending rightward, click to set at 7 symbols
remediation_light: "Count by 5s until you reach 35. How many 5s is that?"

visual: Same horizontal graph and data. Liam row filled (7 symbols). Emma, Noah, Olivia rows empty.
guide: "Finish the picture graph. Use the data table and complete the graph."
prompt: "Set the lengths for all remaining categories."
student_action: Click to set Emma (4), Noah (5), Olivia (8)
on_correct: "You built the whole graph. Each symbol shows 5, and you used skip-counting to figure out how many."
remediation_light: "Check the table for each person's number. Count by 5s to find how many symbols."

visual: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Horizontal. Student's completed "Books Read This Month" graph: Liam=7 symbols (35), Emma=4 symbols (20), Noah=5 symbols (25), Olivia=8 symbols (40). Key visible. Data Table NOT visible.
guide: "Use your graph to find how many FEWER books Emma read than Olivia."
prompt: "How many FEWER books did Emma read compared to Olivia?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 15, 20, 40, 60]
answer_rationale:
- 20 = Correct (40 - 20 = 20)
  - 15 = Calculation error or used wrong values (Olivia minus Noah: 40 - 25)
  - 40 = Used Olivia's value only
  - 60 = Added instead of subtracted (Misconception #6)
on_correct: "20 fewer. Olivia read 40. Emma read 20. The difference is 20."
remediation_light: "'How many FEWER' means find the difference. 40 minus 20 is..."

purpose: Extend M2's binary scale comparison to a three-way pattern. Student toggles between scales 1, 2, and 5 on their own graph data, discovering that "bigger scale = fewer symbols" is a general principle. Builds metarepresentational competence through direct manipulation—the student causes the transformation, making scale a visible choice rather than an invisible given.
visual: Picture Graphs (side-by-side comparison). Student's completed 1:5 horizontal "Books Read" graph displayed. Scale toggle control with three options: "Each ⭐ = 1," "Each ⭐ = 2," "Each ⭐ = 5." Defaults to scale of 5. Data Table visible as stable anchor (Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40). Key updates dynamically. Toggle sequence: 5→1 (Olivia: 8→40 symbols), 1→2 (Olivia: 40→20 symbols; half-symbols appear for odd-divided values), 2→5 (return to original).
guide: "You built this graph with scale of 5. But what would it look like with a different scale? Try it."
prompt: "Toggle the scale to 1. What happens?"
student_action: Click scale toggle from 5 to 1. Graph animates: symbols multiply (Olivia: 8 → 40; Liam: 7 → 35; Noah: 5 → 25; Emma: 4 → 20). Key updates to "Each ⭐ = 1."
guide_2: "Olivia went from 8 symbols to 40! That's a LOT of symbols. Now try scale of 2."
student_action_2: Click scale toggle from 1 to 2. Graph animates: symbols reduce. Key updates to "Each ⭐ = 2."
guide_3: "Fewer symbols — but still more than scale of 5. Toggle back to 5."
student_action_3: Click scale toggle from 2 to 5. Graph animates back to original state. Key updates to "Each ⭐ = 5."
guide_4: "Same 40 books for Olivia every time. Scale of 1: 40 symbols. Scale of 2: 20 symbols. Scale of 5: just 8."
student_action_4: [Multiple choice: Scale of 1, Scale of 2, Scale of 5]
on_correct: "Scale of 5. Bigger scale, fewer symbols — same data."
remediation_light: "Toggle through all three again and count Olivia's symbols each time. 40, then 20, then 8. Which is fewest?"
guide_5: "All three showed the same information. The scale just changes how many symbols you need. Now let's use YOUR graph to answer some questions."
divider: No student graph creation (toggle is observation/exploration only)
design_notes: Student toggles through all three scales in sequence (5→1→2→5). The full cycle makes scale of 5's efficiency feel earned, not asserted. Half-symbols appear naturally at scale of 2 for odd-valued results. Guide does NOT call attention to this—it's background observation. Students already learned half-symbols in M2. Animation between states should be smooth (~0.5s). The toggle becomes inactive after the MC question is answered. The data table stays visible throughout as the stable reference point. The graph transforms; the data doesn't.

visual: Data Tables (Numeric Display). "Weekend Screen Time (minutes)" — Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25. 4 categories. Horizontal layout.
visual_2: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Horizontal. Axis along bottom, labeled 0–45 in 5s. All 4 bars empty.
guide: "This bar graph is horizontal—the bars go sideways. But look at the axis along the bottom. Same skip-counting by 5s. What comes after 35 on this axis?"
prompt: "What comes after 35 on this axis?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 36, 40, 45, 30]
on_correct: "40. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15... 35, 40, 45. Same pattern, just running sideways."

visual: Data Table: "Weekend Screen Time (minutes)" — Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25. Empty horizontal bar graph template. Scale marked 0, 5, 10... up to 45.
guide: "Now a bar graph, also horizontal. Read the data, set the lengths of the bars to complete the graph."
prompt: "Create the bar graph. Set all four bars."
student_action: Hover and click to set Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25
on_correct: "You created the bar graph independently. Horizontal or vertical—you've got this."
remediation_light: "Count by 5s as the bar grows: 5, 10, 15, 20... Stop when you reach the value from the table."

visual: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating). Horizontal. Student's completed "Weekend Screen Time" bar graph (Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25). Saturday and Sunday bars highlighted. Data Table NOT visible.
guide: "How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?"
prompt: "How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?"
student_action: [Multiple choice: 5, 35, 65, 70]
answer_rationale:
65 = Correct (35 + 30 = 65)
  - 5 = Error: Subtracted
  - 35 = Used Saturday only
  - 70 = Calculation error
on_correct: "65 minutes in all. Saturday has 35, Sunday has 30. 35 plus 30 is 65."

visual: Both completed horizontal graphs visible (Books picture graph, Screen Time bar graph).
guide: "You made both kinds of graphs today. Here's one last question. Picture graphs use symbols. Bar graphs use bars. What do these graphs have in common?"
prompt: "What do these graphs have in common?"
student_action:
[Multiple choice:
  - "They both use the same scale"
  - "They both use symbols"
  - "They look the same"
  - "They both have 5 categories"]
on_correct: "Both use scale of 5. Picture graphs show it with symbols—count by 5s. Bar graphs show it on the side. Same scale, different format."

visual: Final graphs remain briefly.
guide: "You made picture graphs and bar graphs, both with scale of 5. You used skip-counting to figure out symbols and bar heights. Let's see what you know."
